When you navigate the reader, every timeline event that shares a scripture reference with your current chapter starts to **glow** in the accent color. Open *John 2* and the Cana miracle marker lights up; open *Acts 2* and Pentecost lights up.

## Click to jump

Click any marker to open its detail card below the timeline. The card lists the year, an era badge, a category icon, a one-sentence summary, the people and places involved, and **clickable scripture refs** — tap a ref to pivot the reader to that passage.

## Filters & search

The chip strip toggles categories (narrative, prophecy, war, covenant, miracle, council, martyrdom, writing). The search box up-right filters by title or summary. Both compose with zoom — narrow to *prophecy* and zoom to ERA for a prophetic-history bird's-eye view.

## Dating note

Dates follow conventional scholarship — Thiele for the monarchy, standard consensus for NT events. Pre-Abrahamic dates are approximate; ranges shown in the detail card reflect the uncertainty.

articles.title: Jewish Study Tools
articles.body:
# Jewish Study Tools ה

The **TORAH** tab in the right rail gathers the weekly Jewish lectionary, Hebrew-calendar context, holiday awareness, and Daf Yomi into one panel — designed to sit next to the reader as a quiet, beautiful companion to whatever passage you're studying.

## Today's Hebrew date

At the top, a date strip shows the **approximate** Hebrew date in Hebrew script (using the Cardo serif), its transliteration, and the year (e.g. 5786). The date is prefixed with **≈** because CODEX currently uses a simple anchor-based approximation rather than a true lunar-conjunction calculation — expect up to a day or two of drift, especially around the year boundary at Tishrei 1. A precise calendar engine is on the roadmap.

## This week's parsha

The central card shows **Parashat HaShavua** — the weekly Torah portion. You'll see the Hebrew name in large serif type, its transliteration, the English meaning, the Torah portion range, and the traditional haftarah reading. **Every reference is clickable** and will jump the reader to that passage. Use the **PREV / NEXT / JUMP TO TODAY** pills to walk the 54-parsha annual cycle without changing what you have open.

## Holiday awareness

If a major holiday is within 14 days, a **gold-trimmed card** appears with the Hebrew name, English transliteration, date, and clickable scripture readings. Otherwise a quiet line tells you which holiday is next and how far out it is.

## Calendar reference

Two collapsible sections let you browse the full **12 Hebrew months** (with Gregorian approximations and notes) and the **complete list of major holidays** (Rosh HaShanah through Tisha B'Av) with all their readings.

## Daf Yomi

When the Daf Yomi reading-plan module is available, today's daf is shown as a chip you can copy or paste into an external Talmud lookup. (CODEX does not yet ship Talmud text — Sefaria deep-links are planned.)

articles.title: Public Data API (v1)
articles.body:
# CODEX Data API (v1)

CODEX exposes a small, read-only **public REST API** over its bundled study modules. Build dashboards, browser extensions, Alfred workflows, hardware displays, or research scripts against it. All endpoints are `GET`, return JSON, and are CORS-open.

**Base path:** `/api/v1`

## Endpoints

- `GET /api/v1/health-public` — `{ ok, version, modules_count }`. Safe for monitoring; reveals no key or usage info.
- `GET /api/v1/modules` — every bundled module's metadata in one shot.
- `GET /api/v1/strongs/:id` — Strong's lexicon entry. `H<n>` Hebrew or `G<n>` Greek.
- `GET /api/v1/crossref/:ref` — TSK cross-references for a verse (e.g. `gen.1.1`, `john.3.16`).
- `GET /api/v1/timeline?from=-2000&to=2000&category=narrative&era=patriarchs` — filter the biblical timeline.
- `GET /api/v1/parsha?week=current` — current or specified ISO-week parsha.
- `GET /api/v1/search?q=love&translation=kjv` — **stub**. Server-side full-text search is planned for Phase 5.6; CODEX currently searches client-side. The endpoint returns an explanatory `note`.

## Rate limits

100 requests per minute per IP. Every response sets `X-RateLimit-Limit` and `X-RateLimit-Remaining`; exceeding returns `429` with a `Retry-After` header.

## Example

```bash
curl http://localhost:3000/api/v1/strongs/H1
curl http://localhost:3000/api/v1/crossref/gen.1.1
curl 'http://localhost:3000/api/v1/timeline?from=-100&to=100'
```

## What's mock vs real

Real endpoints back the lexicon, cross-references, timeline, parsha, and module enumeration directly from the bundled JSON. `search` is a **documented stub** until the full-text index ships.

See [`API.md`](../../API.md) for the complete reference, error shapes, and response examples.

articles.title: Word Study (Λ)
articles.body:
# Word Study Λ

The **WORD** tab in the right rail is a deep-dive on a single Greek, Hebrew, or English word — the lexicon entry, where it lives in scripture, and the theological weight it carries.

## Triggering a study

- **Click any word** in the reader — if it has Strong's data wired up, the panel opens to that lemma.
- **From the verse menu**, choose **Word Study** to pivot from a verse you're reading.
- **From the Strong's panel**, opening a Strong's number broadcasts to Word Study too, so both panels stay in sync.
- **Search box** at the top of the panel — type a word (`love`) or a Strong's number (`G25`, `H157`).

## What you see

1. **Hero** — the original-language word in large Cardo serif, transliteration, Strong's number, and gloss.
2. **Semantic range** — bullet points parsed from the lexicon definition.
3. **Frequency** — a horizontal bar chart by book, computed from your own library via `CODEX_SEARCH`. The more chapters you've opened, the richer this becomes.
4. **First occurrences** — five clickable verses, snippet included; tap to jump straight to the reader.
5. **Related words** — an AI lexicographer call returns related Greek, Hebrew counterparts, and antonyms with their Strong's numbers. Cached per word for 30 days; only fires when an AI engine is configured in Settings.
6. **Why it matters** — a one-paragraph theological synthesis in a serif pull-quote. Also cached.
7. **Key passages** — surfaced for high-frequency lemmas.

The last studied word is remembered between sessions.

articles.title: Module Marketplace (⌬)
articles.body:
# Module Marketplace ⌬

The **MARKET** tab in the right rail is CODEX's storefront for **community-contributed study modules** — lexicons, cross-reference databases, commentaries, reading plans, dictionaries, atlases, language packs, and more.

## How modules work

A *module* is a plain JSON file with a `meta` envelope (`id`, `type`, `version`) plus typed payload. The runtime — `modules.js` — fetches, validates, and caches modules in IndexedDB, so once installed they work offline. The full envelope and every supported `type` are documented in `SPEC.md`.

## Sections in the panel

- **Installed** — every module currently in your IndexedDB cache. Each row shows type, version, and install date, with a **Remove** button.
- **Featured** — a curated grid of the most useful community modules.
- **Browse by category** — filter chips for Lexicons, Cross-Refs, Commentaries, Reading Plans, Dictionaries, Maps, Timelines, Language Packs, and Devotionals.
- **Add by URL** — paste any URL pointing to a valid module JSON, supply the expected module id, hit **Install**. The runtime validates the `meta` envelope and refuses anything malformed.
- **Add by file** — drag-drop a `.json` module onto the dropzone (or click to pick) to install from disk. Great for previewing a module you're authoring before publishing.

## Module detail view

Click any card or row to open a full detail page: long description, source link, license, install or remove button, and a collapsible **Raw JSON** preview.

## Curated index & contributions

The Featured and Browse lists are sourced from `data/module-index.json` — a curated catalog that ships with the app. Entries marked **COMING SOON** are placeholders describing planned modules and aren't yet hosted; they're shown so you can see what's on the roadmap. To contribute a new module to the curated index, see [`CONTRIBUTING.md`](../../CONTRIBUTING.md) in the CODEX repo.

articles.title: How CODEX Compares (⚖)
articles.body:
# How CODEX Compares ⚖

The **COMPARE** tab in the right rail shows a side-by-side, honest assessment of CODEX against the seven most-used Bible study apps: **Logos**, **e-Sword**, **YouVersion**, **Sefaria**, **Olive Tree**, and **Blue Letter Bible**.

## What's in the panel

1. **Hero stat strip** — the four numbers that matter most: price ($0), offline (100%), license (open source), AI-native (yes).
2. **Feature matrix** — 29 features × 7 apps. Cells are colored ✓ accent, ⚠ amber, ✗ dim, or `$$$` for paywalled. Hover any cell for a sourcing note.
3. **Price chart** — horizontal bars scaled to a $5,000 ceiling. Logos stretches across; CODEX is a single `$0` pill.
4. **Coverage radar** — an 8-axis SVG radar (Original Languages, AI Features, Offline, Reading Experience, Study Depth, Mobile, Customization, Community). Click legend chips to toggle apps.
5. **Love / Frustration cards** — aggregate sentiment per competitor, pulled from Reddit, App Store reviews, and blog posts (2024–26).
6. **Roadmap** — the gaps we know about: full Strong's, full TSK, Capacitor native apps, Linux Tauri, more translations.

## Honesty policy

CODEX is younger than every competitor on this list. Some rows we lose — **translations count** (YouVersion has 2,800+, we have 43+), **mobile native** (Capacitor builds are still cooking). Those losses are visible in the matrix and called out in the roadmap.

## SHARE THIS

The button at the bottom copies the full feature matrix as a Markdown table to your clipboard. Paste it into Reddit, Discord, or a tweet.

## Found a wrong row?

The entire dataset lives in `compare.jsx`. Open it, fix it, and send a PR via [`CONTRIBUTING.md`](../../CONTRIBUTING.md). Sourcing this honestly is a community job.

articles.title: BabelForge — Translation Lab ⌬
articles.body:
# BabelForge — Translation Lab ⌬

**BabelForge is for serious translators AND for the gospel-sharer who wants Genesis in cowboy English. Both are valid. Both serve the text.** Rigor levels keep you honest; voice templates set you free.

Open the **BABEL** tab in the right rail to start. Spin up a project — pick a voice, set a rigor level, choose a book, and start drafting verse-by-verse with the AI as your co-translator (or as your strict editor if you'd rather translate by hand).

## The spirit of BabelForge

Inspired by fansubs and fandubs but at scholar level. The idea: a Tanakh in Wall Street terms to introduce Genesis to a banker friend; a translation in modern Mexican slang for the homies at carne asada; a serious, careful translation in a language that hasn't been done yet; a strict-rigor English revision; a King-James-cadence version with modern spelling. **All of it valid. All of it serving the text.**

## Voices (20 ship out of the box)

From **Modern Scholar** (NRSVue-adjacent, careful, neutral) to **Messianic Jewish** (Yeshua, Mashiach, Ruach HaKodesh preserved) to **Picture-Book Bible** (for 6-year-olds and the read-aloud parent) — and on the playful side: **Wall Street Tanakh**, **King James (the Other One — NBA voice)**, **Para los Carnales** (warm Mexican-Spanish code-switch), **Gospel Noir**, **Cowboy Frontier**, **Pirate's Bible**, **Haiku Mode**, **Adventurer's Codex (D&D)**, **Sitrep Scripture**, **Pulpit Cadence**, **Zen Koan**, and more.

Each voice ships with a sample line so you can see it in action before you commit. Or pick **Custom Voice** and write your own system prompt — describe the voice in your own words and the AI will follow your spec.

## Rigor — the honesty dial

Four levels of automated source-faithfulness checking:

- **Strict** — AI refuses to add or omit. Proper nouns must appear. Doctrinal anchors (God, Christ, sin, salvation, covenant…) must survive. Word-count drift > 30% is a hard fail.
- **Balanced** — additions are flagged as warnings, not failures. A recognized native equivalent (Yeshua for Jesus) is fine.
- **Loose** — style flexes freely. Theology and event-sequence must still hold.
- **Free** — no automated checks. Pure creative mode. Use with care.

Every saved verse gets a badge — **✓ source-faithful**, **⚠ flagged**, or **✗ rigor-failed**. You always see the truth.

## The three-pane editor

- **LEFT** — original-language crib (Strong's-derived where available).
- **CENTER** — base translation (KJV etc. — your starting point).
- **RIGHT** — your draft, plus AI notes (info / warn / fail).

Toolbar lets you regenerate with AI, switch to manual mode, lock a verse so bulk operations skip it, and jump verse-by-verse. **Translate next 10 verses** lets the AI plow through a passage; **Re-check rigor on all** re-runs validation if you've raised the strictness mid-project.

## Exporting

Three ways out:

- **CODEX Translation JSON** — drops into `data/modules/translations/{id}.json` so it can be re-imported as a real loaded translation in the app.
- **Markdown** — for sharing, printing, or pasting into a doc.
- **Share URL** — a `#bf=…` URL hash that recreates the translation on any device.

Community browsing is coming — eventually you'll be able to share translations with the wider CODEX community.

## A note on reverence

Every voice prompt — even Pirate Bible — explicitly preserves proper nouns, sequence of events, subject/object relationships, and theological meaning. We paraphrase the vehicle, never the cargo. A playful translation is still a faithful translation; that's the whole point. **Bring people to the gospel however they'll listen — but never lie about what it says.**

articles.title: VOX — Voice & Prayer (◉)
articles.body:
# VOX — Voice & Prayer ◉

The **VOX** tab in the right rail turns CODEX into a reading companion that can speak any passage aloud in dozens of languages — and a prayer guide that holds reverent space across more than twenty traditions.

Every tradition is welcome here. Christian, Catholic, Orthodox, Jewish, Messianic, Sufi, Gnostic, Hermetic, Quaker, New Age, interfaith — all valid prayer formats.

## Reading mode

1. **Source** — read the current chapter, just the current verse, or paste any custom text (any language).
2. **Voice** — VOX auto-picks a voice matching your active translation's language. The dropdown groups every system voice by language; voices marked **★** are Natural / Neural / Enhanced / Premium quality (Apple voices on macOS & iOS, Google WaveNet on Android, etc.).
3. **Speed, pitch, volume** — sliders save per-voice automatically.
4. **Play / Pause / Stop** — large 44px touch targets. **VERSE ◀ / ▶** jumps the reader by one verse.
5. **Live highlight** — as TTS speaks, VOX dispatches `codex:vox-progress` with the current verse reference so the reader (or any panel) can highlight along.
6. **Read aloud from any verse** — open the verse menu on any verse and choose **Read aloud** to start playback from that verse without touching the panel.

### Voice quality

Native `speechSynthesis` is zero-cost, offline-capable, and ships with the OS. Quality varies:

- **macOS / iOS** — Apple's Natural / Enhanced / Premium voices (English, Spanish, French, German, Italian, Hebrew, Arabic, Chinese, and more).
- **Chrome / Edge on Windows** — Microsoft Neural voices when installed.
- **Android** — Google TTS, including high-quality WaveNet voices.
- **Linux** — typically eSpeak (robotic but functional).

The **✦ ENHANCE VOICE** button is reserved for a future cloud-TTS upgrade (OpenAI / ElevenLabs / etc.) via a server endpoint.

## Prayer mode

Switch to **✶ PRAYER** to browse 20+ prayer formats, each rendered as a tradition-tinted card. Pick one and CODEX shows you the prayer in big serif type with structured sections (invocation / petition / declaration / refrain / silence / doxology). Tap **✶ GUIDE ME** and the voice engine reads it aloud at a contemplative pace — pausing between sections, holding true silence for the duration each silence-section specifies.

The shipped traditions include: **The Lord's Prayer**, **Lectio Divina**, **The Examen**, **Breath Prayer / Jesus Prayer**, **Rosary — Joyful Mysteries**, **Compline (Night Prayer)**, **Trisagion**, **Akathist — Glory to God for All Things**, **Shema Yisrael**, **Amidah (Standing Prayer)**, **Birkat HaMazon**, **Modeh Ani**, **Al-Fatiha** (for interfaith study), **Hesychast Prayer Rope**, **Sufi Dhikr**, **Thomasine Reflection** (Gospel of Thomas), **As Above, So Below** (Hermetic), **Land Acknowledgment & Thanksgiving**, **Quaker Silence**, **Shema with Yeshua** (Messianic), **Invocation of the Inner Light** (New Age contemplative), and a **Free-form Petitionary** slot for AI-composed prayer.

### Generate a personalized prayer

Open the **Free-form Petitionary** card. Pick a tradition chip (Christian, Catholic, Orthodox, Jewish, Messianic, Sufi, Gnostic, Hermetic, Quaker, New Age, Interfaith), describe your situation — *anxiety before a job interview, grief over a friend, gratitude after recovery* — and press **✦ COMPOSE**. CODEX calls the configured AI engine to draft a reverent ~150-word prayer in that tradition's conventions. **▶ READ ALOUD** then reads it back at a contemplative pace.

### Prayer timer

For fixed-length practices — five-minute lectio, twenty-minute centering prayer — tap a timer chip. A gentle *Amen* is spoken when the timer ends.
